options: A storage-engine rollback segment unwinds the committed transaction
Compensating actions, like issuing a refund to reverse the charge
A two-phase-commit coordinator aborts and rolls back all participants
A read-repair pass reconciles the divergent replicas
why: Add compensating actions (like refunds when inventory can't be found) to undo already-completed steps — the core idea behind the saga pattern.
more: Rollback segments and 2PC abort only help inside a single ACID database; once payment was charged in a separate service, the only way back is a semantic compensating transaction.

options: Applying updates, versioning, and migrations to an already-running workflow
Keeping the accumulated workflow state from growing without bound
Guaranteeing a given step executes exactly once despite retries
Compressing and resizing media before it is uploaded
why: The deep dives cover workflow versioning/migrations, keeping state size in check, handling external events, and exactly-once steps. Media compression belongs to the blob-upload pattern, not workflow execution.

options: Martin Kleppmann's 'Turning the Database Inside Out' on stream processing
Jimmy Bogard's 'Six Little Lines of Fail' on the hidden difficulty of a short step sequence
Adam Wiggins' 'The Twelve-Factor App' on building stateless services
Kleppmann's 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' chapter on consistency
why: HI cites Jimmy Bogard's 'Six Little Lines of Fail', whose premise is that distributed systems make even a short sequence of steps surprisingly hard to do reliably.

options: Roughly 1:1, with reads and writes growing in lockstep
Around 10:1, often climbing far higher for content-heavy apps
About 2:1, since most users both read and post
Already 100:1 from day one for nearly every product
why: The standard ratio starts near 10:1 but often reaches 100:1+ for content-heavy apps like Instagram or YouTube.
more: 100:1 is real for mature content apps, but it is the destination, not the typical starting point.

options: Mostly bad software you can debug and refactor your way out of
Ultimately physics — CPU instructions per second, memory limits, disk I/O bounds
Largely a poor programming-language or framework choice for the hot path
Usually insufficient logging and observability hiding the real bottleneck
why: It is usually not a software bug you can debug away; it is physics, where CPU, memory, and disk I/O hit hard constraints that more code cannot overcome.

options: Add external caching first, then shard the data, then tune the database
Optimize within the DB, scale it horizontally with replicas and sharding, then add external caching
Buy a CDN first, then attach read replicas, then revisit indexing
Shard the database first, denormalize the schema, then upgrade to bigger hardware
why: First optimize within the DB (indexing, hardware, denormalization), then scale horizontally (read replicas, sharding), then add external caching layers (application cache + CDN).

options: Adding and tuning indexes for the hot query paths
Upgrading the database server hardware (CPU, memory, disk)
Applying denormalization strategies to cut joins
Serving responses from a CDN and edge caches
why: Indexing, hardware upgrades, and denormalization are the in-database optimizations. CDN and edge caching are a later external layer, not work done within the database.

options: Replication lag delaying reads on the database replicas
A thundering herd or cache stampede where many simultaneous misses hit the DB
A hot partition key overloading one shard on the write path
CDN cache poisoning serving a corrupted cached response
why: This is the cache stampede or thundering herd: when a popular entry expires, many requests miss at once and hammer the backing database to rebuild it.

options: Reads bypass the database entirely, so only writes touch it
Bursty, high-throughput writes with heavy contention are hard to design around
Writes are served from cache by default, hiding their true cost
Writes avoid disk I/O, so they scale linearly with more CPU
why: Read-side tools like replicas and caching are familiar, but bursty high-throughput writes with contention are genuinely hard, and many design choices make them worse.

options: Vertical scaling together with deliberate database engine choices
Sharding and partitioning to spread the write load across nodes
Absorbing bursts with queues and shedding load when overwhelmed
Batching writes and using hierarchical aggregation to reduce volume
why: HI combines exactly these four: vertical scaling plus DB choices, sharding plus partitioning, burst handling via queues plus load shedding, and batching plus hierarchical aggregation.

options: Spread the data across hundreds of shards from the outset
Stay within a single-server, single-database setup and optimize it first
Push writes to a global CDN edge before they reach origin
Add read replicas to take pressure off the primary
why: First scale within a single-server, single-database setup using vertical scaling and write optimization before reaching for more hardware or distribution.
more: Read replicas help reads, not writes — writes still funnel to the primary.

options: Splits rows across many machines using a partition key
Splits a table by columns so different attributes live in separate stores
Replicates the entire table to every node for read locality
Buffers writes in Redis before persisting them to the database
why: Horizontal sharding splits rows by key; vertical partitioning splits a table by columns or attributes so different fields can live and scale separately.

options: Pre-split every key across multiple sub-partitions up front
Split only the hot keys dynamically as they emerge
Apply backpressure and shed writes to the hot key under load
Replicate the hot key to read replicas to absorb the traffic
why: The hot-key deep dive offers two remedies: split all keys (pre-split) and split hot keys dynamically. Shedding writes or leaning on read replicas does not solve a write-side hot key.
more: Replicas only help reads, so they cannot relieve a write hot key; load shedding drops data rather than scaling the key.

options: Anything over about 1KB that is read only occasionally
Anything over about 10MB that does not need SQL queries
Every image, regardless of how small or how it is queried
Only very large files above roughly 1GB in size
why: If something is over ~10MB and doesn't need SQL queries, it should probably live in blob storage like S3 rather than in the database.

options: 99.9 percent durability, about three nines
99.99 percent durability, about four nines
99.999999999 percent durability, about eleven nines
100 percent durability, contractually guaranteed against any loss
why: S3 advertises 99.999999999% (eleven nines) of durability, with effectively unlimited capacity and per-object pricing.

options: Keeping file metadata in a relational database alongside the blob
Routing the file bytes through your application servers as a proxy
Serving downloads through a CDN sitting in front of blob storage
Compressing files on the client before they are uploaded
why: The 'server as a proxy' anti-pattern routes bytes client to server to S3 and back; it works for small files but breaks down as files grow large.

options: Presigned URLs so clients upload directly to blob storage
CDN-based downloads that pull from blob storage, not your servers
Resumable uploads with parallel transfers and progress tracking
Storing the whole file as a BLOB column inside Postgres
why: Presigned URLs (direct-to-blob), CDN downloads, and resumable parallel uploads all keep bytes off your servers. Storing the file as a DB BLOB is the opposite — it wrecks query, backup, and replication performance.

options: Provision a larger application server to hold the partial upload
Use resumable multipart or chunked uploads so a failure does not restart from zero
Move the file metadata into a SQL database for durability
Disable the CDN so uploads take a more direct network path
why: The 'fails at 99%' case is solved by resumable uploads: chunked or multipart transfers let an upload resume from the last good part rather than restart entirely.

options: A read phase followed by a separate write phase
Immediate acknowledgment followed by background processing
An authentication step followed by an authorization step
A compression step followed by an encryption step
why: The pattern splits the request into immediate acknowledgment (validate, enqueue, return a job ID in milliseconds) and background processing done by separate workers.

options: The database eventually runs out of rows to write
Web servers and load balancers enforce ~30-60s timeouts, so the request may never complete
Browsers abort any request that takes longer than ten seconds
Server-side code is unable to generate PDF documents at all
why: Beyond the spinner, the 45s report often won't finish synchronously because servers and load balancers enforce ~30-60s timeout limits and cut the request off.

options: The fully rendered PDF document in the response body
A job ID, after validating the request and pushing the job to a queue
A connection string pointing the client at a read replica
The raw queue offset together with the assigned worker's IP
why: The web server validates, pushes a job to a queue (Redis or RabbitMQ), and returns a job ID within milliseconds while workers do the heavy lifting and update status.

options: Recovering gracefully from repeated job failures
Preventing the same job from being processed twice
Managing queue backpressure when work arrives faster than workers drain it
Choosing which CDN region should serve the output
why: The deep dives cover repeated failures, preventing duplicate work, queue backpressure, mixed workloads, and job dependencies. CDN region selection belongs to the large-blobs pattern.

options: A single background thread running inside the web server process
Separate worker processes that poll the queue, grab pending jobs, run them, and update status
The client browser executing the long-running task locally
A read replica that picks up and auto-executes queued jobs
why: Separate worker processes continuously poll the queue, grab pending jobs, run the time-consuming work, and update job status in a database — fully decoupled from the web server.

options: Hash the long URL and take the first seven characters as the code
Encode a unique counter in Base62 so every code is unique by construction
Generate random seven-character strings and retry whenever they collide
Use the long URL itself, URL-encoded, as the short code
why: Prefer counter + Base62: uniqueness is guaranteed and no collision handling is needed.
accept: Hash-and-truncate is workable but requires a UNIQUE constraint plus collision retries; keep that constraint anyway as a safety net even with the counter approach.

options: A SQL AUTO_INCREMENT column incremented on every single write
A single Redis atomic INCR, with each server claiming ~1000 IDs at a time
A UUID generated independently on each application server
A millisecond timestamp combined with a server identifier
why: Redis INCR is atomic and fast; batching ID ranges (grab ~1000 at once) cuts network round-trips so the counter isn't a per-request bottleneck.
more: Losing a few counter values on failover is fine — you only need uniqueness, not a gap-free sequence.

options: 301 permanent, since browsers cache it and that lowers server load
302 temporary, so requests keep flowing through your server and you can add analytics
301 permanent, primarily because it is better for search-engine ranking
Either one, since the redirect type has no practical impact
why: HI prefers 302: the browser keeps hitting your server, so you retain control and can later add click analytics.
accept: 301 is defensible when minimizing load matters more than visibility, but the browser caches it and you lose per-click insight.

options: On the server, after the client streams up one large upload
On the client, using S3 Multipart Upload with a presigned URL per part
Inside the database, storing each chunk as a BLOB row
Not at all, since chunking is unnecessary for files under 50GB
why: Chunk on the client (~5-10MB parts) and use S3 Multipart Upload: CreateMultipartUpload, a presigned URL per part, then CompleteMultipartUpload.
more: Server-side chunking defeats the purpose — the big file still flows through your servers first.

options: The original filename supplied by the client
A combination of the user ID and an upload timestamp
A SHA-256 hash of the content, used as a fingerprint
A database auto-increment ID assigned on upload
why: Use a fingerprint — a SHA-256 hash of the content. It enables dedup (same content already stored) and resumability (which chunks are present), neither of which a filename or timestamp can provide.

options: Switch to larger fixed-size chunks to reduce the number of boundaries
Use content-defined chunking with a rolling hash, as in Rabin fingerprinting
Restrict large uploads to off-peak overnight windows
Turn off chunking entirely for any file that has been edited
why: With fixed offsets an early insert shifts every boundary so all chunks look changed. Content-defined chunking sets boundaries from a rolling hash of the content, so only the edited region's chunks change.

options: A long-running database transaction holding a row lock for the duration
A Redis distributed lock with a TTL that auto-expires if the user abandons checkout
A boolean reserved column flipped on hold with no expiration
Optimistic concurrency with retries and no explicit hold
why: The strong answer is a Redis lock keyed by seat with a ~10 minute TTL so abandoned holds auto-release.
accept: A status-plus-expiration column scanned by a cron job is a defensible 'good' fallback, but the final DB write under ACID is what actually guarantees no double-booking.

options: Provisioning much larger database instances for the on-sale window
A virtual waiting queue that admits users into the booking flow in a controlled trickle
Caching the seat map more aggressively at the edge
Sharding the seat inventory by seat ID across nodes
why: A virtual waiting queue protects the booking path from the thundering herd by admitting users in controlled batches — the move that signals senior or staff level.

options: LIKE with wildcards is not valid SQL syntax for text columns
A leading-wildcard LIKE forces a full table scan, so use Elasticsearch instead
LIKE quietly returns incorrect or partial result sets
LIKE only operates on numeric columns, not on text
why: A leading-wildcard LIKE can't use a B-tree index, so it scans the whole table — too slow at scale. Elasticsearch's inverted index is the right tool; add query-result and edge caching.

options: Pure fan-out-on-write, precomputing every follower's feed for all authors
Pure fan-out-on-read, recomputing every viewer's feed from raw posts on request
Hybrid: precompute feeds for normal users, pull celebrity posts at read time, merge
Store no feeds at all and fully recompute from scratch on every request
why: The strong solution precomputes (pushes) into per-user feed tables for normal accounts but pulls celebrity posts at read time and merges, avoiding the millions-of-writes-per-celebrity-post explosion.

options: Increase the cache TTL so the hot entry is recomputed less often
Replicate the hot entry across several cache nodes to spread the read load
Consolidate onto a single, much larger cache node with more memory
Stop caching the hottest posts and read them straight from the source
why: DynamoDB and single cache nodes struggle with hot keys. Replicating the hot post across multiple cache nodes spreads reads instead of concentrating them on one partition.

options: A dedicated Kafka topic per user that the receiver's server consumes
Redis Pub/Sub with one channel per user, so the receiver's server is subscribed
A shared database table that both chat servers continuously poll
Consistent hashing so both users always pin to the same chat server
why: The strong answer is Redis Pub/Sub with one channel per user, which sidesteps consistent-hashing complexity.
more: A Kafka topic per user is an anti-pattern — Kafka isn't built for hundreds of millions of topics.

options: Force every client onto raw TCP and rely on its delivery guarantees
Persist to a durable Inbox before publishing, require client acks, and use per-chat sequence numbers
Publish each message twice to raise the odds it is delivered
Let the load balancer detect failures and retry the delivery
why: Persist undelivered messages in an Inbox (deleted on ack) and have clients detect gaps via per-chat sequence numbers piggybacked on heartbeats, with a polling backstop, covering Pub/Sub's dropped messages.

options: Enforce a single global total order across every chat
Enforce strict per-chat ordering using server-side locks
Stamp NTP-synced server receipt times and let clients sort, tolerating rare pop-in
Order messages by each sending device's local clock
why: Don't pay for strict ordering. Stamp messages with NTP-synced server receipt time and let clients sort; users tolerate rare reordering. Client device clocks can't be trusted.

options: Wait until the user's next message reveals the connection is gone
Use application-level heartbeats, since TCP keepalive reacts too slowly
Periodically ping the database to infer whether the client is alive
Proactively close and re-establish all connections every minute
why: TCP keepalive intervals are too coarse, so use application-level heartbeats to spot dead connections quickly and derive 'last seen' from active connections rather than per-heartbeat DB writes.

options: DynamoDB with on-demand capacity absorbing the write spikes
An in-memory geospatial store like Redis using geohashing or a quadtree
A Postgres table with a B-tree index on the latitude and longitude columns
One S3 object per driver, overwritten on each location update
why: That write rate in DynamoDB would cost a fortune (>$200k/day), and a B-tree can't answer 2D proximity queries. Redis with geohashing handles high-frequency writes and radius queries in memory; add adaptive client-side update intervals.

options: A simple delay queue that re-enqueues the offer after a timeout
Durable execution such as Temporal or Cadence driving the offer-timeout-reoffer flow
A cron job that periodically scans rides for unanswered offers
Client-side timers on the driver app that trigger the next offer
why: This is the multi-step-process pattern. Durable execution (Uber authored Cadence) survives crashes and reliably advances offer to 10s timeout to next driver, which a fragile delay queue can't guarantee.

options: Only the user's display name needs to be re-derived server-side
userId, timestamps, and the fare estimate — derive each from the session, server, or DB
Nothing needs re-checking once the client is authenticated
Only the payment token should be re-validated server-side
why: Never trust client-supplied identity or money fields: pull userId from the session or JWT, set timestamps server-side, and re-read the fare from the DB by fareId.

options: Write every click to Postgres and run GROUP BY aggregations at query time
Stream into Kafka or Kinesis, aggregate in Flink, write to an OLAP store like ClickHouse or Druid
Have a cron job periodically aggregate clicks accumulated in a CSV file
Count clicks on the client and report rolled-up totals to the server
why: Ingest into a stream, aggregate in Flink, and write to an OLAP database optimized for analytical queries. Using one transactional DB for both ingest and analytics is the anti-pattern.

options: Stop tracking the viral ad until its traffic subsides
Append a random suffix to the partition key (AdId:0..N) and SUM the parts after stripping it
Provision a single much larger Kafka broker to absorb the hot ad
Switch the aggregation pipeline to a fan-out-on-read model
why: Salt the key with a random suffix so the hot ad's events spread across partitions; the aggregator strips the suffix and sums per AdId. This is classic hot-shard remediation.

options: Trust the streaming path, since a well-tuned stream does not drop events
Lambda architecture: also dump raw events to S3 and run a nightly Spark batch as source of truth
Re-run the same streaming job a second time and compare the outputs
Extend the cache TTL so aggregated results survive transient loss
why: Use a speed layer (Flink) for near-real-time plus a batch layer (Spark over an S3 data lake) as the authoritative recompute. Reconciling the two catches drift and loss in the streaming path.
